text: The video shows the anatomy of the left chest with the lung reflected inferiorly, revealing the arch of the aorta and the left main pulmonary artery. Posteriorly, the pulmonary artery is visible towards the apex. Inferiorly, the inferior ligament is seen with lymph nodes reflected through it. The inferior pulmonary vein is pointed out, located just below the left main bronchus.

The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament, revealing the inferior pulmonary vein. The posterior hilum is dissected, showing the left main stem bronchus and the pulmonary artery. In the fissure, the branching pulmonary artery is visible, with the lingular artery in the foreground and a branch to the posterior upper lobe and the superior segment pulmonary artery in the background.

The surgeon reflects the lung posteriorly, revealing the heart and the phrenic nerve. The internal mammary artery and vein, also known as the internal thoracic vessels, are visible. Posteriorly, the vertebral body is observed.

The surgeon palpates the sympathetic chain located behind the parietal pleura with an instrument. Towards the apex of the chest, the thoracic inlet is visible, along with the pulsatile subclavian artery.

The surgeon palpates the T-one nerve root with an instrument, located near the first intercostal muscle between the first and second ribs. The first rib is touched, and following its resection, the anatomy of the thoracic inlet is visible, including the subclavian vein, the divided anterior scalene muscle, the subclavian artery, and the brachial plexus posteriorly.
